general_knowledge: The Tufted Duck is a diving duck found throughout the Northern Hemisphere. It is a regular winter visitor to Singapore, arriving from September to April.
identification_tips: Males distinctive with glossy black head featuring drooping crest, white sides, and black back. Females brown with smaller crest. Both sexes have orange feet and yellow eyes.
habitat: Freshwater and brackish lakes, reservoirs, and coastal waters. Prefers deeper water bodies where they can dive for food.
behaviour: Highly gregarious, often seen in flocks of 10-100+ birds. Dives to feed, sometimes staying underwater for 30+ seconds. Males perform elaborate courtship displays in spring before migration.
diet: Aquatic invertebrates, mollusks, seeds, and plant matter. Omnivorous with preference for animal matter.
feeding_habits: Dives 2-8 meters deep to forage on lake bottoms for snails, mussels, and aquatic insects. Most active feeding at dawn and dusk.

general_knowledge: The Yellow-vented Bulbul is a common and widespread resident throughout Singapore. It is one of the most abundant bird species in urban and suburban areas.
identification_tips: Medium-sized brownish bulbul with distinctive yellow undertail coverts (vent). Lacks crest. Thin pointed bill. Often seen in small flocks.
habitat: Urban and suburban gardens, parks, scrubland, secondary forest edges, and cultivation. Highly adaptable to human-modified landscapes.
behaviour: Active, noisy and gregarious. Often seen in small groups of 3-8 birds. Constantly on the move in low to mid-story vegetation. Vocal with varied calls including harsh scolding notes.
diet: Omnivorous. Fruits and berries form bulk of diet, especially figs. Also feeds on insects, spiders, and occasionally flowers.
feeding_habits: Forages in trees and bushes, actively gleaning insects from foliage. Visits flowering and fruiting trees. Will come to fruit feeders in gardens.

general_knowledge: The Common Kingfisher is a small, brilliantly coloured kingfisher found throughout Singapore. It is resident year-round and frequents freshwater habitats.
identification_tips: Unmistakable bright turquoise-blue upperparts contrasting with rufous-buff underparts. Compact body with large head and long pointed bill. Orange-red feet.
habitat: Freshwater and brackish water bodies including streams, rivers, reservoirs, and coastal mangrove areas. Prefers shallow water with overhanging vegetation or perches.
behaviour: Solitary or in pairs. Perches on low branches or cables overhanging water. Hunts by waiting and diving into water for small fish. Territorial and returns to favourite perches.
diet: Primarily small fish (2-5 cm) caught by diving. Also takes small crustaceans, aquatic insects, and tadpoles.
feeding_habits: Sits on low perch 0.5-2m above water, scans for fish movement, and dives with a splash. Successful catch rate high. Swallows fish head-first.

general_knowledge: The Black Bulbul is a resident fruit-eating bird found in forest and wooded areas throughout Singapore. It is particularly common in nature reserves.
identification_tips: Entirely black or blackish-slate plumage with slight gloss. Relatively large bulbul with rounded body shape. Slight crest when alert. Bright red or orange undertail coverts distinctive when fanned.
habitat: Primary and secondary forests, wooded areas, and well-vegetated parks. Prefers higher canopy levels. More forest-dependent than other local bulbuls.
behaviour: Usually in pairs or small flocks of 3-5 birds. Movements jerky and erratic. Often perches at mid to high level in trees. Males may display with tail fanned to show red undertail coverts.
diet: Predominantly frugivorous. Feeds on figs, mistletoes, and other small fruits and berries. Occasionally supplements with insects.
feeding_habits: Forages in fruiting trees, often visiting same tree repeatedly during fruiting season. Will hang upside-down to reach fruit. Particularly active at dawn and dusk.

general_knowledge: The Barn Swallow is a common winter visitor to Singapore, arriving from September to April. Large numbers pass through during migration.
identification_tips: Sleek build with steel-blue upperparts and rufous-buff underparts. Deeply forked tail with elongated outer feathers. Rufous patch on forehead and face.
habitat: Open habitats including gardens, parks, coastal areas, and open water. Often seen perching on wires and cables. Hunts in open skies.
behaviour: Highly gregarious, especially on migration, roosting in hundreds or thousands. Graceful fliers, constantly hawking for insects in flight. Acrobatic manoeuvres.
diet: Exclusively insectivorous. Feeds on small flying insects caught in flight including flies, mosquitoes, small beetles, and flying ants.
feeding_habits: Forages entirely on the wing, often at height. Swallows prey while flying. Very active feeder, covering large distances daily. Follows water edges hunting insects.

general_knowledge: The Common Hill Myna is a resident species found in wooded areas, parks, and gardens throughout Singapore. It is a highly vocal and conspicuous bird.
identification_tips: Black overall with distinctive yellow wattles behind eyes and yellow bill. Large yellow patches on wings visible in flight. Stocky build with upright posture.
habitat: Wooded areas, secondary forest, well-vegetated parks and gardens, and urban areas with large trees. Highly adaptable.
behaviour: Usually in pairs or small groups of 3-5 birds. Vocal with loud, clear whistling calls and mimicry of other species. Hops along branches and on ground.
diet: Omnivorous. Feeds on fruits (figs, insects, seeds, and occasionally small reptiles). Often visits fruiting trees in mixed foraging flocks.
feeding_habits: Forages in trees and on ground. Visits fruiting trees and will come to feeders. Often seen moving about in early morning and late afternoon.
